title: The Iran War Turned Asia’s Fragile Energy Dependence Into an Emergency | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: The Iran war and February–June 2026 closure of the Strait of Hormuz converted Asia's long-known Gulf-energy dependence into an acute economic emergency, knocking ~0.7pp off 2026 GDP growth and triggering a rapid regional pivot to solar, nuclear, EVs, Russian crude, and non-Gulf LNG. But grid underinvestment, deepening reliance on Chinese clean-tech supply chains, a coal rebound, and ASEAN's failure to coordinate threaten to slam the policy window shut.

themes.theme: Macro damage from the oil shock

insights.claim: The Asian Development Bank forecasts a 0.7-percentage-point drop in 2026 GDP growth and inflation rising to 5.2 percent if oil prices hover around $96 per barrel for the year.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Quantifies the macro drag from sustained ~$96 oil on Asian growth and inflation—a benchmark for positioning in EM Asia rates, FX, and equities.
insights.quote: The Asian Development Bank forecasts a drop in GDP growth of 0.7 percentage points in 2026 and inflation rising to 5.2 percent if oil prices hover around $96 per barrel for the year.

insights.claim: The Philippines declared a national energy emergency in March 2026 after local diesel and petrol prices more than doubled, while Bangladesh bought LNG spot cargo at nearly three times normal benchmark prices.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Shows the price pass-through hit import-dependent EM Asia hardest and unevenly—watch fiscal/subsidy strain in the Philippines and Bangladesh as sovereign-credit signals.
insights.quote: Bangladesh, which has purchased liquefied natural gas (LNG) spot cargo at nearly three times normal benchmark prices

insights.claim: Asian governments responded to the shock with emergency demand-rationing—Bangladesh, South Korea, Thailand and Vietnam imposed energy rationing; Indonesia and Malaysia ordered work-from-home; Pakistan and the Philippines moved to four-day work weeks.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: The breadth of emergency rationing measures gauges how exposed each economy was; their rollback signals where pent-up activity may rebound.
insights.locator: The emergency measures governments rolled out in March and April

insights.claim: Before February 2026 the Middle East supplied roughly 60 percent of Southeast Asia's crude oil imports and about a third of its gas, while Japan was even more exposed with 95 percent of its oil arriving through the same Gulf routes.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Baseline exposure figures explain why a Hormuz closure is existential for Japan and SE Asia and frame the scale of diversification needed.
insights.quote: Japan was even more exposed, with 95 percent of its oil arriving through the same routes.
insights.locator: Before the war, the Middle East supplied

insights.claim: Iran has signaled intent to impose tolls on ships using the Strait of Hormuz regardless of any final peace deal, and energy analysts warn full normalization of Middle Eastern energy exports is unlikely before mid-to-late 2027 at best.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: A structural toll plus a 2027 normalization timeline keeps a persistent risk premium in oil and shipping freight rates even after a ceasefire.
insights.quote: Energy analysts warn that a full normalization of Middle Eastern energy exports is unlikely before mid-to-late 2027 at best.
insights.locator: Whether that translates into a genuine recovery

insights.claim: Chinese solar exports to the Philippines roughly tripled in Q1 2026 versus a year earlier, making it one of the region's fastest-growing solar markets as rooftop installations surged on high utility bills.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Direct demand signal for Chinese solar manufacturers and a leading indicator of distributed-solar adoption across crisis-hit SE Asia.
insights.quote: Chinese solar exports to the country roughly tripled in the first quarter of 2026 compared to a year earlier
insights.locator: The Philippines offers a striking illustration

insights.claim: Five ASEAN members—Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand and Vietnam—are now actively pursuing nuclear power, with Malaysia targeting its first reactor by 2031 and Vietnam and Bangladesh advancing Russia-backed plants.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: A regional nuclear revival favors reactor vendors (notably Russia's Rosatom) and uranium/fuel-cycle names, though timelines are long and execution uncertain.
insights.quote: If current plans hold, nearly half the region could have nuclear capacity by the 2030s.
insights.locator: Nuclear power is getting a serious second look

insights.claim: Several Southeast Asian governments including Indonesia, the Philippines and Vietnam moved quickly to secure Russian crude as Gulf supplies dried up, and are looking at non-Gulf LNG from the United States and Australia as lasting alternatives.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Demand redirection toward Russian crude and U.S./Australian LNG reshapes trade flows and supports those exporters at the Gulf's expense.
insights.quote: Several Southeast Asian governments, including Indonesia, the Philippines, and Vietnam, moved quickly to secure Russian crude as Gulf supplies dried up.
insights.locator: The search for alternative oil suppliers

insights.claim: The IEA's Southeast Asia Energy Outlook 2026 projects renewable capacity—120 gigawatts in 2024—could nearly triple by 2035 under current policies, or grow fivefold if announced targets are met.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Frames the upper-bound SE Asia renewables build-out (up to ~600GW), a multi-year demand pool for solar, wind, and grid equipment.
insights.quote: renewable capacity—at 120 gigawatts in 2024—could nearly triple by 2035 under current policies or grow fivefold if newly announced targets are met
insights.locator: A new report by the International Energy Agency

themes.theme: Obstacles that could close the window

insights.claim: If Southeast Asia fails to diversify quickly, the IEA warns its energy import bill could rise to $245 billion by 2035, tripling from $80 billion in 2024.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Quantifies the recurring fiscal/external-balance drag of inaction—a tail risk for SE Asian current accounts and currencies.
insights.quote: its energy import bill could rise to $245 billion by 2035, tripling from $80 billion in 2024

insights.claim: Southeast Asia's grids are badly undersized: transmission and distribution would need to more than double in length by 2050, annual grid and storage spending would have to climb from $13 billion to $50 billion, and the long-stalled ASEAN Power Grid alone carries a ~$27 billion price tag through 2040.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Grid and storage capex is the binding constraint on the renewables build-out—the real investable bottleneck behind the solar/wind headlines.
insights.quote: annual grid and storage spending would have to climb from $13 billion to $50 billion over the same period
insights.locator: The region’s electricity networks are badly undersized

insights.claim: China's dominance of renewable-tech supply chains—solar panels, batteries, EVs—means Southeast Asia's energy diversification will deepen its reliance on China in geopolitically and economically dangerous ways.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: The pivot away from Gulf oil dependence trades one strategic dependence (Middle East) for another (China clean-tech)—a key second-order geopolitical risk.
insights.quote: Southeast Asia’s energy diversification strategy will deepen reliance on China in geopolitically and economically dangerous ways

insights.claim: The loss of Gulf LNG supplies has pushed fuel switching back toward coal, which still supplies half of Southeast Asia's electricity and contributed to an estimated 330,000 premature deaths in the region in 2024.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: An energy crisis intended to spur clean energy is paradoxically boosting cheap domestic coal—bullish near-term coal demand in Indonesia despite transition rhetoric.
insights.quote: the loss of Gulf LNG supplies has pushed fuel switching—back toward coal
insights.locator: And then there is the coal problem

insights.claim: ASEAN's consensus-based structure has repeatedly blocked energy coordination—the May 2026 summit failed to agree a joint energy plan, Indonesia's regional petroleum reserve proposal collapsed for a third time, and Vietnam and Cambodia declined to endorse measures that would complicate their China dealings.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Institutional gridlock means diversification will be national and uneven, not regional—raising execution risk and limiting collective bargaining power on supply.
insights.quote: Indonesia’s proposal for a regional petroleum reserve—tabled at three consecutive summits—collapsed again.
insights.locator: The ASEAN leaders’ summit in May

insights.claim: China and India alone contribute roughly 40–45 percent of incremental global GDP growth, with other major Asian economies adding another 10–12 percent, so a sustained Asian energy crisis could substantially compress global growth over the next two decades.
insights.speaker: Joshua Kurlantzick
insights.so_what: Asia's outsized share of marginal global growth makes its energy resilience a systemic variable for the world economy, not a regional sideshow.
insights.quote: China and India alone contribute roughly 40 to 45 percent of incremental global GDP growth
insights.locator: The stakes of this moment extend beyond Asia.
